title: Trees to Flows and Back: Unifying Decision Trees and Diffusion Models
abstract: Decision trees and diffusion models are ostensibly disparate model classes, one discrete and hierarchical, the other continuous and dynamic. This work unifies the two by establishing a crisp mathematical correspondence between hierarchical decision trees and diffusion processes in appropriate limiting regimes. Our unification reveals a shared optimization principle: \emph{Global Trajectory Score Matching (GTSM)}, for which gradient boosting (in an idealized version) is asymptotically optimal. We underscore the conceptual value of our work through two key practical instantiations: \treeflow, which achieves competitive generation quality on tabular data with higher fidelity and a 2\times computational speedup, and \dsmtree, a novel distillation method that transfers hierarchical decision logic into neural networks, matching teacher performance within 2\% on many benchmarks.
summary: This paper provides a mathematical unification of decision trees and diffusion models, showing they are different limits of the same underlying optimization principle. It introduces new methods, TREEFLOW and DSM-TREE, which leverage this connection to achieve faster and more accurate generative modeling for tabular data.
main_problem: The paper seeks to bridge the mathematical and conceptual gap between discrete, hierarchical decision trees and continuous, dynamic diffusion models.
main_result: The authors establish a formal bidirectional mapping between trees and flows, proving that gradient boosting is an asymptotically optimal solver for a unified objective called Global Trajectory Score Matching (GTSM).
method: The work uses dyadic refinement to transform discrete partitions into continuous-time SDEs and introduces two new algorithms: TREEFLOW for generative tabular data and DSM-TREE for tree-to-neural network distillation.
model_or_system: The framework unifies discrete-time Markov processes (decision trees) and continuous-time stochastic differential equations (diffusion models) through a shared optimization principle.
key_observables: TSTR (Train Set Test Re-use) accuracy, Wasserstein distance, Correlation Error, Shannon entropy decay, and runtime efficiency.
important_parameters: Tree depth (D), number of leaves (L), feature dimensionality (d), and the continuous-time limit of dyadic refinement.
main_assumptions: Assumes entropically homogeneous SDEs, well-separated modes in initial distributions, and that intermediate tree splits do not change conditional densities at original levels.
figures_summary: Visual metaphors of tree-to-flow correspondence; dendrograms showing discovered hierarchies in diffusion models; entropy decay curves comparing trees and diffusion; and performance benchmarks for TREEFLOW and DSM-TREE.
paper_structure: The paper begins with a theoretical unification of trees and flows via GTSM, followed by proofs of convergence and complexity for the proposed algorithms, and concludes with experimental validation on synthetic and tabular datasets.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: A Visual Metaphor for Trees to Flows and back. We connect the discrete structure of a decision tree (left) with the continuous dynamics of a flow process (right). We establish a a formal correspondence, enabling an analysis of boosting via flows, and imbuing generative models with tree-based inductive biases.

Figure 2: Implicit tree structure discovered from a trained diffusion model on the 4-Corners dataset using the corrected time-domain clustering method. (a) The original labeled data clus- ters, representing the leaves of the hierarchy at t = 0. (b) The discovered hierarchical structure (dendrogram) obtained by tracking the forward SDE trajectories of each cluster and performing agglomerative clustering. The vertical axis is not rescaled; it directly represents the discovered merger time t. (c) A visualization of the system’s state at the internal node time t = 0.50. The plot shows the particle positions at this intermediate time, generated by solving the learned reverse PF-ODE. The...

Figure 3: Comparison of information decay on MNIST. (Top) The normalized entropy of a decision tree (by depth) and a diffusion process (by time) follow near identical trajectories. (Bottom) Visual prototypes show analogous information loss, with tree prototypes averaging over data subsets and diffusion prototypes showing a single instance being noised. (Small Gaussian noise is added to tree prototypes to ease visual distinguishability; zoom for clarity.

Figure 4: TREEFLOW compared against baseline generative models across a suite of tabular bench- marks. We evaluate on four axes: Utility (TSTR Accuracy ↑), Fidelity (Wasserstein Distance ↓), Structure (Correlation Error ↓), and Efficiency (Runtime ↓). TREEFLOW achieves highest TSTR accuracy on 3/5 benchmarks, lowest Wasserstein distance on 4/5 benchmarks, and lowest correlation error on 3/5 benchmarks, while being 2X faster than TabDDPM. Zoom for clarity (or see Figure 15).

Figure 5: Classification accuracy of the DSM-TREE model compared to its teacher (Base Tree). DSM-TREE matches or exceeds teacher performance on 4/5 datasets, with +3.7% improvement on Heart Disease, demonstrating successful knowledge transfer of complete hierarchical structure. Zoom for clarity. A larger version is available in Figure 14 with full results in Table 2.
